title: Your First 2-Minute Presentation
description: Prepare and deliver a short structured presentation on any topic you know well. This session covers preparation, delivery, and reflection.

steps.instruction: Choose a topic you know well. Read about presentation structure: what are the three parts of a structured presentation and what does each accomplish?
steps.expectedOutcome: You understand the opening (hook + preview), body (3 key points), and closing (summary + call to action) structure
steps.hint: Tell them what you'll tell them, tell them, then tell them what you told them

steps.instruction: Identify your audience: Who are they? What do they already know? What do they care about? Read about audience adaptation: how does knowing your audience change what you say and how you say it?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can describe your audience's knowledge level and interest, and adjust vocabulary and examples accordingly
steps.hint: An expert audience needs less background; a general audience needs more context and analogies

steps.instruction: Write a brief outline — just 3 bullet points for the body, plus an opening hook. Now practice delivering it while looking at yourself in a mirror or recording yourself. Read about voice and articulation.
steps.expectedOutcome: You have delivered the presentation at least once and noticed your pace, volume, and clarity
steps.hint: Most first-time speakers speak too fast when nervous — deliberately slow your pace

steps.instruction: Review your recording (or your memory). Notice: Did you make eye contact? Did you pause between points? Read about nonverbal communication: what do body language and eye contact communicate to an audience?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can identify at least one nonverbal element to improve (eye contact, posture, gestures) and explain what it communicates
steps.hint: Eye contact signals confidence and creates connection; avoiding it signals nervousness or lack of preparation

steps.instruction: Deliver the presentation again, incorporating your improvements. Afterwards, invite feedback from a real or imagined listener. Read about active listening: how does listening to feedback improve future delivery?
steps.expectedOutcome: You have completed a full second delivery with conscious attention to one improvement, and can articulate what you would change next time
steps.hint: Specific feedback ('your opening was engaging') is more useful than general feedback ('it was good')
